title: Mules For Hire: Why I Became One
preview: “Corruption, violence, and poverty was an everyday reality in my homeland of Mexico. Papa did the best he could for his family but, it didn’t afford the luxuries a 17-year-old dreamed of. Desperation for a better life caused me and my father to make some...
body:
“Corruption, violence, and poverty was an everyday reality in my homeland of Mexico. Papa did the best he could for his family but, it didn’t afford the luxuries a 17-year-old dreamed of. Desperation for a better life caused me and my father to make some decisions that would affect us all.”

Aña Marie Cruz, 23 years old Tijuana, Mexico

II

was raised in a multi-generational home where family relationships are very close. We were lower class and considered poor with a capital P. I have three brothers Daniel, Jorge and Juan, two sisters Amelia and Elisa, and grandmama and grandpapa. Mexico is a very patriarchal culture where the men have more authority than women. They are the primary providers and decision makers. Therefore, whatever pappa said that’s how it was going down. On the other hand, women are expected to be self- denying and totally dedicated to the family. It is their duty to be the happy little homemaker (cooking cleaning up and catering to the male members). You are to be the “good wife,” mother, sister or girlfriend. We are supposed to wait on them hand and foot and feel a sense of pride and satisfaction for this honor. Submissiveness was how my sisters, and I were raised.
The culture of machoism is a generational curse for our young girls and women. My mama has suffered her mama and now her daughters. This has been almost impossible to change. Let me break it down. It’s an attitude that men think they are superior to women just by being born. To them we are second class citizens. This is so dangerous because it limits females on how they should act and think and limits control over our lives and body. Ladies do we really want a man to tell us what to think, how to dress and have our legs open and ready when they want sex? Hell nah!
Mama was raised under these traditional family rules. She wanted to be a teacher. That was her dream growing up. Her siblings played the students. They would sit at the kitchen table and practice reading and writing the alphabets in Spanish and English. Her mama always said she was sure to be a good teacher one day. Dad, stood by looking and shaking his head. Making hurtful comments was all he did. “You’re not gonna be no teacher.” You’re gonna marry and be an obedient wife, housekeeper and mother.”
There was nothing mama could say. A wife is supposed to agree with her husband. Of course, the brothers were encouraged to continue their education or seek job opportunities in the construction industry. Mama’s dad stole her dreams right out of her. Hearing that negativity over and over you start to believe it and she did. Mama told us she would never forget how terrible it felt not being able to fulfill something you always wanted. The only crime was being a daughter and not a son. Papa just kept saying, “You’re my little girl and the little lady of the house.” “That’s all you need to think about.” My role had been defined before I was even born. I think she gave in and gave up.
My grandmother on the other hand was more resistant and a little bit stronger. She was the one who told me “estudia mucho,” to study hard. This way you never have to depend on a man. She definitely ahead of her time! Her father used machismo as an excuse to be abusive and controlling. He would say very mean things to his wife and daughters. He never put his hands on her. Make no mistake ladies, just because a man doesn’t hit you upside your head doesn’t mean you are not being abused. Education was seen less important for the daughters. The kids were trained to either become breadwinners or homemakers. There was no, in between and nothing else to think about.
Well, my grandmother decided she would think. One night at the age of 16 yrs. old she quietly left home and ran away. There was a town not far away where her mother’s sister lived with her family. When father found out she was gone he was furious and of course blamed his wife for her bad behavior. He sent word for her to never return even though her mother cried and begged.
There was nothing he could do anyway because she wasn’t ever coming back. Working at a roadside flower shop helped to contribute a little money for her new family. During this time, she never gave up on completing her education. She had no money to spare. But, through an organization she contacted, they offered her a scholarship to study at a local school. Working and studying hard paid off. She pushed past the established gender role where education was not for girls only for boys. You didn’t have to put up and shut up. Sexism didn’t have to be your birthright. I guess that’s where I got my bold behavior from. This bold thinking would soon lead me into a world of risky and dangerous behavior.
Today, some of these traditional views are changing and young women are pushing back. That second in command shit to a man ain’t happening. Although, some women still follow the domestic role. The new generation of young women would be highly insulted if a man demanded they have to do all the household chores, cooking or any other tasks requested of them. Unfortunately, we were taught to be obedient when it came to the machismo man (male dominance.)
Remember the old wedding vows? The minister would say, “Do you promise to cherish and obey til death do us part?” That’s a long ass time of servitude. Who came up with this anyway? We already know man did. It’s funny the man doesn’t vow to obey his bride. Thank goodness brides everywhere said, “No mas.”
There was no challenging these rules. You were expected to know your place and not step outside of it. Two sets of rules applied one for the boys and one for the girls. My brothers were praised more even if they did something wrong, while we took the blame more times than often. Girls must adhere to stricter rules while boys are allowed to have more freedom. You know the saying, “Boys will be boys.” This was our way of life, and we learned not to go against tradition. When girls turned 18 you would think we were grown and free at last. We could now move away from home. Not gonna happen! When you’re living in poverty daughters don’t have that privilege. Independent living and secondary education are costly. It’s always about the money. So, it’s home sweet home until my prince charming comes along and marries me.
As the years went by and we were getting older, Papa saw all the working wasn’t paying off. When he would get home late, I could hear him and mama having some heated discussions. Some of it was about money and papa leaving. I knew it was serious because it was on her face. She would always assure us that everything was fine, and we didn’t have to worry. It didn’t fool me, and I saw right through it.
Sure enough, weeks later papa held a family meeting. He told us he would be leaving soon to go to the U.S. We couldn’t believe that the anchor of our family was going away. He confessed that the money he was making could not feed or clothe us. What little money he saved would be used to pay the Coyote’s. Coyotes are people who guide migrants across the border. They are usually closely tied with cartels and other criminal organizations. Papa told us when he found work and a place to stay, he would let us know and send money back. Mama would seek work wherever she could. He instructed my oldest brother Daniel to also step up and find odd jobs after school to keep the family going.
We were all worried especially my grandparents. They had heard stories of men leaving their families and never returning. Each day, we waited to hear word of his arrival which was supposed to be Chicago. This is where a lot of illegals were hired to work in factories. Weeks later, he let us know that he made it to the big city and was working. We gave thanks that his journey was a successful one. The factory that employed him had no problem with hiring workers from Mexico. It was to their benefit. They could pay them less and work them more hours. They knew these men needed them to make whatever money they could to send back home. Plus, these were menial jobs that most Americans wouldn’t even take.
Papa made just enough to send home and whatever was left went for his food and housing. He found out the American dream was not the American dream. Our quality of life changed a little but not by much even with Daniel working odd jobs. The main concern was making sure we provided the medicine needed for the grands. Every now and then we would have extra meat, ice cream or other personal essentials. This may not sound like a lot to you but for us it was a treat.
Everything was going well as could be, and then the money and letters stopped coming. We didn’t know if papa was sick, arrested, or dead. We sent a letter to the address where he was staying with several other men. It came back undeliverable. There wasn’t anything else we could do but wait. Weeks went by and still no word. There was no one to get answers from. He had left Mexico as an illegal. He also conspired with criminals to leave the country.
A few days later while we were eating dinner 197 there was a quiet knock at the door. Daniel went to see who it was. He peeked out the window first. It could have been trouble. We sat quietly to find out who or what was waiting to come in. He slowly opened the door and let out a loud scream. There was papa slumped over on the ground. We could hear him yelling out! We all ran to the door. He needed help into the house and my brothers brought him in and sat him up in a chair. It was clear that he had been through a rough time. He was almost unrecognizable. He was thinner, unshaven, and tired. Things didn’t work out the way it was planned.
The Government or U.S. Immigration started an investigation at the factory. Numerous workers were found undocumented. So, the company was fined, and everyone was returned to their Country of origin. The next few weeks was tough. The little money my brother was providing was not enough to make ends meet. It was going to be a difficult thing for papa to do but, he would have to go back to the farm he was slaving for before he left. We knew his pride was in the way, but money was desperately needed. Fortunately, Papa was able to return to work.
The Los Grande Agricultural Farm is privately owned and one of the biggest and most powerful ones. They had hundreds of employees. The workers were treated like crap (low pay, long hours, few benefits). A truck would pick the men up early morning at a location in town. They would transport them there and back about 30 miles total each day. The family, especially my grandparents were worried about the conditions he was exposed to. When he arrived home, we could see that his hands and fingers were bruised. It hurt us to see how this labor of love for his family affected him. We knew it was impairing his health and spirit. Something had to change. I was determined to make it happen.
There were several girls that I hung out with in the neighborhood. They were a little bit more mature than me. Mama and grandmama would say to me, “Esas chicas so malas noticias (those girls are bad news). They figured any girls that were allowed to be seen hugging on boys in public were not the ones I should be associating with. I made excuses to leave the house to be with them. We weren’t far just around the neighborhood. Talk was about boys and teasing each other about who liked who and if there were any kisses shared. There were serious conversations too; about how hard our fathers had to work taking care of us.
One afternoon when school was out, I decided to go into town. Maybe I could find a job at one of the markets. I was taking a chance because Papa would be angry at the thought of his daughter supporting his family. “Heh Aña,” someone was calling my name. It was Lucia. One of the girls from school. She wanted to know what I was doing in town. I told her I was there looking for a job. It was a surprise that she was speaking to me. She was very popular, and I was far from being included in her crowd. I was a nobody who wanted to be a somebody.
Lucia told me to meet her at the market the next day after school and she would try to help me make some money. Everybody knew which kids were poor and which ones, were not. It was public knowledge. I didn’t give it a second thought and agreed to be there. The next day at school she waved at me and said, “See ya later.” I should have been smart enough to know that she had an alternative motive. Why was she being so nice to me?
The day seemed forever to end. When I arrived at the market, she was already there. She introduced me to Carlos a man who was standing next to her. No introduction was needed. I already knew who he was. He eyed me up and down like I was a pork chop. Lucia told me you want to make some money to help your family. I know who your father is. He works on the Los Grandes Agricultural farm. It must hurt you to see him leave early in the morning to meet the truck in town. Then, at night seeing his lifeless body when he gets home. I know you don’t want to see him working his self to death. He painted such a dismal picture that I was willing to do almost anything now.
You see, Carlos recruited students from our school to be mules. They’re used to smuggle or carry drugs across the border. He convinces them that they can make some fast easy money this way. He knows what kids come from poor families and will approach them for sure. Most of the kids do it because they want to be cool. Peer pressure is another reason. While others may have grown up in the drug family culture following in their older sibling’s footsteps. The girls do it because they get jeans, purses, dresses and more. This is a big deal for those teenage girls who want to fit in. It is so important at this age. It promotes self-confidence and conforms to the latest fashion trends. Some of the girls dated narcos (drug dealers). They love the idea of dating a “bad boy” and hanging out with risk-takers. It’s a power thang. What do these kids get in return? Maybe, a false sense of importance and a few dollars in their pockets.
Those being recruited the most are called border crossers. They are generally Mexican citizens with border crossing cards. This allows them to attend school in the United States mostly near the border in San Diego. You probably wouldn’t suspect a teenager on the way to school smuggling drugs in their uniform. The cartels send as many kids as they can across at the same time and hope most of them slide through. If you look nervous, Customs and Border Protection officials can spot you a mile away. You couldn’t display any behavior that makes them give you a second look. Some of them do get caught. Those that make it will get paid.
Carlos already made the case for me and that influenced my decision. I was reminded again that my father worked on one of the roughest farms in Mexico. He had to endure harsh conditions and exploitation to supply produce for the Americano consumers who don’t even know or care about your father. Do you want that for him? Right then, I had a flashback that was disturbing. I remembered the image of my father and how crushed he was after immigration sent him back to Mexico. The next day Lucia pulled me into the bathroom and told me I was to meet Carlos after school at the market. I knew what time it was, and fear immediately took over me.
It was hard to believe I was about to be a mule or a fool and cheap labor for the cartel. I was instructed to meet at a local restaurant in town an hour before departing for school. there would be cartel workers there waiting for me and other girls in the bathroom to prep us for our crossing. This particular restaurant was a cover for the cartel’s dirty business. There were several of them and they all worked together. There were several ways to conceal the drugs. They could be attached to the outside of the body using adhesive tape. It would be placed between the cheeks of your ass, or your thighs. If you were a little chunky, it could be placed between the rolls of fat in your stomach or taped to your back. Everyone met at different locations to avoid unwanted attention. This is how it was done during my time.
The next morning, I told mama my teacher wanted me to arrive before class to help her. I left and made my way to the restaurant and waited inside the bathroom with more girls. We all knew what we were there for. Minutes later, several rough looking women entered and asked all of us for our names. Then, she locked the door. They quickly opened suitcases, and we were told to take our uniforms off. These women meant business and not playing, and we all complied. One of them studied my body after I undressed. It was a weird feeling having someone checking me up and down. It was decided the bags of mota (weed) would be taped to my back and taped across my breast under my bra. Our blouses were a little roomy so the drugs would not be detected. The tape was so tight on my back and breast I could hardly breathe. She said I was a fragile innocent looking girl and I would go unnoticed. We were told to hurry and get dressed. My orders were to get to the bridge and cross with the rest of the others. We were all aware of our mission. It was too late to back out now.
Hundreds of people crossed the border and back every day and night (workers, families, students). All we had to do was just mingle in with the rest of the crowd. Lucia had already explained to me that some of the Border Patrol were under cover incognito working for the cartel. Thank goodness we all crossed safely that day. We still had to deliver the packages before school and get paid. There was a drop off point not too far where Carlos was waiting.
This would not be the last time I entered the doorway to this drug shack. There was still one major problem that had to be addressed. How was I going to explain to my parents where this extra money was coming from. Mama had already been told that my teacher wanted me to help her prepare handouts before class. So, all I had to do now was say she wants me to tutor several students having trouble with reading English. The teacher informed me that payment would be made for my services. My mother would have to convince my father. His pride might be a problem having his daughter contributing money to the family. He did finally give in and was convinced that it was an honor the teacher chose me to tutor her students. With the extra dollars we were doing a little bit better and able to purchase clothing, food and other essentials. Still, we were living at the poverty level. Even though I was making money at the border and tutoring at school; it was not a good idea to appear that we might be doing too well. It would draw unwanted attention to my family. I had to be careful at all times. The money I made wasn’t anywhere near what the cartel was getting. It’s always the little people that make the ones in power the richest.
I learned everything worldwide is built on supply and demand. Business was growing and Carlos informed us that more mules would be needed. It was going to be our responsibility to bring in more crossers. Lucia and her friends didn’t necessarily need the money. They were in it because it was cool. There were no worries where their next meal was coming from. Survival was not their motive. It was extra money to splurge on whatever they wanted. Their status in the community was much higher than mine. I wasn’t as brave or bold enough to hang out with or have a boyfriend in the cartel. Lucia and her crew were able to lie and sneak around. They came from families that weren’t as strict as mine. I was pretty much under a microscope. These girls were the real (“Esas chicas son malas noticias), that my grandmother referred to. The girls I hung out with were no fresca (not cool) at all. At times I kind of envied the way they lived. They were free and uninhibited.
Carlos surprised us one morning showing up at the restaurant. He was spreading the word that it was time for all of us to get more kids to smuggle. This was a directive from the top. None of my friends knew what I was doing, and I wanted to keep it that way. I told him I didn’t know anyone that would be willing to do this. Before I could get it outta my mouth, he grabbed and shook me. He said, “I was in the big leagues and wasn’t a little naïve girl anymore.” He told me to find them by next week or else! Next week came and I had nothing. Another top lieutenant heard of my unwillingness to cooperate. This time when Carlos saw me there was no mercy. He was waiting for me near the bathroom and immediately threw me against the wall so hard I almost passed out. Carlos let me know another top boss Mr. Garcia was not happy that I was not cooperating.
Garcia is the owner of the Las Grande Agricultural Farms. I had no idea that he too was part of the cartel, wow. This is the farm papa slaved for. He sent a message that I needed to get with the program, or things were going to get worse for my father. I was worried they would kill him for sure. Or maybe tell him his goody good tutoring daughter wasn’t helping the teacher at all.
I was too far in now and had no choice but to enlist some more kids. I was going to depend on my best friends to save me from this nightmare. There is a saying “There’s no honor among thieves.” The men and women in the cartel have kept this organized crime going at any expense. There was no honor. They didn’t care who they hurt. The only thing they knew was greed, money and power. I went to Lucia and explained what happened with Carlos and the situation I was in. She told to hurry and get those other kids. They are not playing Aña. Then out of nowhere she asked me if I was a virgin. Of course, I was still a virgin. I’m telling you give Carlos whatever he wants. He has a lot of power and can protect you and your father. She said, “It’s just sexo.” “No seas un bebe.” (it’s just sex, don’t be a baby.) I should have figured she was a la puta (a whore). Was I going to be one too?
I managed to talk my three friends into helping me. After, they heard the trouble I was in, they stepped up. They were shocked to find out what I had been doing. They understood why because they were living in poverty as well. It was surprising that they would easily become involved in this craziness. Their excitement was more about the money and the things they would be able to buy. It was going to be up to them to figure out how to tell their parents where the money was coming from. That was one less problem I didn’t have to deal with.
Carlos, met with the new kids to explain what they were supposed to do and the location of their reporting stations and the time to be there. They were told about the taping process and where the drugs could possibly be placed on the body. There was an actual demonstration so they would have an idea of what to expect. Lucia and I were both there to emphasize how important it was to pay attention to facial expressions and body language. It could raise red flags and become a dead give- away they’re hiding something. It was a serious warning for them. This was no joke.
The chances of getting caught were almost non-existent. The bottom line was to blend in with all the people crossing. Carlos promised we would all be protected if we got in trouble. He was really only protecting his drug money. I never really thought about it but metaphysically, a mule defined was who we all were. It was kids being used and abused by a corrupt group of gangsters. Meeting was over and everyone was dismissed but me. I was told to stay behind. He brought up my father again about protecting him. If I was nice to him, he would be nice to me. I could hear Lucia’s voice telling me to give Carlos whatever he wanted. Right then and there I wanted out. There was no way I was going to have sex with him, or was I?
Carlos, was probably in his early 30’s. He had a thick mustache with a hairy body, piercing eyes that seem to look right through you. Overall, just hard looking (like you would see in a mug shot). It was a scary thought to envision his naked body sweating all over me. In this case sometimes you gotta do what you gotta do for the family. I closed my eyes and prayed this would be over quickly.
I jumped up in bed shaking and nauseated. Oh my God, it was only a bad dream. I realized then, how deep in shit I was. These people were ruthless and were not your friends. The main concern was my father. Carlos continued his threats to make life for him a living hell. Resistance to his sexual advancements only angered him even more. Something had to give, and it did.
When papa was working in Chicago, he made a friend who was also from Mexico. They were both deported at the same time but kept in touch. It seems he eventually made it to California as a migrant worker with the help of a family member. The law allows employers to bring foreign workers into the United States for temporary or seasonal employment. His friend wanted to help him as best he could. Information was shared with papa and what he would need to do to make it happen. It was decided that he had to take this chance for the sake of the family. He would not survive on the Los Grande farm any longer.
Once the requirements were met, he was approved as an undocumented migrant worker in the United States. Immigrants are essential to the California labor force, and this need helped him get in. Whatever happened, it would be better than where he was now. His friend also told him he had fulfilled the residency needed to take the naturalization exam. He was excited to tell him that he passed and was now a citizen. His days on the farm were over. Driving taxi’s, was his new job. This gave papa hope that we would all be able to join him in the future.
As for me, promises of teen riches were about to be just another dream. Once Carlos found out my father was no longer in Mexico, he could no longer hold threats over my head. That bastard found another way to get revenge. The next day I crossed the bridge as usual on my way to school. I knew something wasn’t right because one of the guides that sometimes crosses with us was following me. They make sure we delivered the drugs and didn’t chicken out. I saw him out the corner of my eye. I saw him motion to one of the guards as he pointed to me. I was almost at the end of the bridge when the border patrol grabbed and pulled me to the side. The other kids kept going. They knew better than to stop because that would bring attention to them.
I was arrested and told they suspected me of smuggling drugs across the border. One of the females took me to an empty room in the office and instructed me to undress. Yep, Carlos had set me up! He was angry because he couldn’t get the cono (pussy). That morning, I never made it to the restaurant. I guess the woman that taped us didn’t think anything about me showing up on time. Several times I had been late before. I never made it and she had no time to let anyone know that Aña was missing in action. Too many questions could bring unwanted attention to herself or anyone else involved. So, she let it go. No drugs were found. They apologized and let me go. After that, I was scared that Carlos was going to come after me but, he never did. I found out later why.
Lucia’s boyfriend heard that no one was going to mess with me. She told me I knew too much. As long as I kept my mouth shut, I was good to go. I had outsmarted them, and any further attention would not be good for the organization. It would be an embarrassment if anyone knew what really happened. In the end, I was actually like a mule stubborn. First, I didn’t give into Carlos and second my virginity was still intact. I didn’t surrender to the pressures and the threats from Carlos either. I really was stubborn as a mule!
Lastly, what happened to all the money that was made? I hid it in a special place. Can’t tell you where or with whom. I have to protect this person and still keep my cover as well. We used a little bit at a time to keep us afloat while papa was gone. I did get a job in town at one of the markets. So, my family never found out what I had done. Lucia stopped smuggling and just hung out with her narco boyfriend getting high. My three best friends were able to get out because they were minors. They never got busted.
As for my family, the United States eventually became our home. It was important for me to fulfill my mother’s dream. I was accepted to a university and earned a degree in Education. I am working as a bilingual teacher in an elementary school. My mother knows that dreams do come true. Her daughter proved it. We are all together now, viviendo nuestras mejores vidas, living our best lives.
Warning, mamas everywhere don’t allow your daughters to become welcome mats. It is understood that every culture has their traditions they abide by. However, no one should succumb to Servitude to another human being. Teaching your daughters to be submissive is counterproductive to her becoming a self-confident, productive, with the ability to think for herself, unafraid to stand up for what she believes in and most importantly self-love. Sometimes, when one person dominates especially for women, power imbalances can be damaging to a relationship. Of course, all situations are different. Just saying, when you speak of waiting on a man hand and foot this is not a healthy or equal union. Remember, abuse can take many forms from physical, emotional, sexual, and psychological.

REMEMBER TO TEACH OUR YOUNG GIRLS.
THEY ARE THE FUTURE TEACHERS OF THE FAMILY.
